intro: Wwise is freemium with a free tier; Ink is free and open source. Ink: Open-source narrative scripting language by Inkle Studios powering 80 Days, Heavens Vault, and Disco Elysium. Has a JSON runtime and Unity/Godot plugins for interactive fiction. Wwise: Professional interactive audio middleware by Audiokinetic. Industry standard for adaptive music and sound design in AAA games, with deep Unity and Unreal integration. Ink fits narrative-heavy games where writers own script files and version control. Wwise fits AAA and mid-tier studios needing top-tier adaptive and spatial audio. On our rubric Ink scores 4/5 for developer experience and 5/5 for transparency, while Wwise scores 5/5 and 3/5. The honest trade-off: Ink's main drawback — Syntax takes getting used to for non-writers; Wwise's — Steeper learning curve than FMOD for audio teams. One point in Ink's favour: Proven on shipped hits like Disco Elysium and 80 Days. For Wwise: The AAA audio middleware gold standard. Ink is explicitly not the right pick for teams whose designers prefer visual dialogue graph editors.
quick_take: Ink is for narrative-heavy games; Wwise is for AAA; decide on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose Ink if narrative-heavy games where writers own script files and version control, and if you want to avoid recurring license costs and are willing to self-host.
verdict_b: Choose Wwise if AAA and mid-tier studios needing top-tier adaptive and spatial audio, and if you accept tighter coupling in exchange for the managed surface.

faq.a: Yes on list price: Ink is free and open source, while Wwise runs on freemium pricing. Keep in mind that Ink shifts costs to hosting, ops, and engineering time. Total cost of ownership can flip depending on your team's capacity.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Ink to Wwise?
faq.a: Migrating out of Ink is straightforward — it's rated low lock-in (OSS scripting language). The harder question is how deeply you commit to Wwise afterward, since it sits at medium lock-in (Industry-standard, portable projects). Plan the forward cost, not just the exit.

faq.q: Which has better developer experience, Ink or Wwise?
faq.a: Wwise scores higher in our rubric (5/5 vs 4/5 for Ink). Transparency is 3/5 for Wwise and 5/5 for Ink. DX scores are rubric-based, not benchmarks, so evaluate against your own toolchain.

faq.q: Is Wwise a good alternative to Ink?
faq.a: They sit in the same category, so yes — Wwise is a plausible alternative for many Ink use cases. It fits best when AAA and mid-tier studios needing top-tier adaptive and spatial audio. Skip it if hobby projects and small mobile games where Unity audio covers the need.
